quick_facts.label: Occupation reference
quick_facts.value: Tea Plantation Manager is listed with NCS Code 1311.08 on a Government of Rajasthan career-guidance portal; it describes the role as supervising and controlling the overall operation of a tea garden.

quick_facts.label: Relevant undergraduate preparation
quick_facts.value: ICAR’s undergraduate-admission bulletin includes B.Sc. (Hons.) Agriculture, B.Sc. (Hons.) Horticulture and B.Sc. (Hons.) Forestry within bachelor’s programmes in agriculture and allied sciences.

quick_facts.label: Sector-specific learning option
quick_facts.value: Tea Research Association (TRA) Tocklai describes its six-month Tea Management diploma as covering tea culture and manufacture, agronomy, plant physiology and breeding, soils, processing, quality, plant protection, HR/grievance redressal, certification and market economics.

sections.heading: What a Plantation Manager does
paragraphs: A Plantation Manager directs day-to-day estate operations. In the tea-plantation reference used by the Government of Rajasthan career portal, this spans coordination from establishing tea plants through the final storage of leaves in warehouses.

In a broader plantation-management example, the National Highways Authority of India describes manager-level work as assessing plantation status, recommending remedial action, preparing site-specific proposals and implementation plans, coordinating implementing agencies, and monitoring delivery. For a farm or estate manager, the exact crop and employer change the work, but planning, field execution, monitoring and coordination are core functions.
bullets: Plan crop and field activities, inspect conditions and act on production or plant-health issues.
Organise workers, supervisors, contractors and external implementing agencies.
Monitor implementation against field plans and maintain operational oversight.
For plantation businesses that process the crop, coordinate production and quality requirements across field-to-factory activities.

sections.heading: Entry routes from agriculture, forestry or horticulture
paragraphs: The most directly aligned entry route is to complete one of the node’s parent degrees—B.Sc. (Hons.) Agriculture, B.Sc. (Hons.) Forestry or B.Sc. (Hons.) Horticulture—and then build practical estate, farm or plantation experience. These three programmes are included by ICAR among bachelor’s programmes in agriculture and allied sciences.

For specialist tea-estate work, a graduate may add crop-specific training. TRA Tocklai’s Tea Management diploma is one example of a sector-focused course that combines field production, processing, quality, plant protection, people management and market topics.

Employers set their own selection criteria. A Government of Uttarakhand Tea Development Board notice for a Tea Estate Manager role, for example, preferred agriculture, forestry, horticulture or science backgrounds and requested substantial prior assistant-manager experience; this is an example of a senior-estate vacancy, not a universal national requirement.
bullets: Start with an agriculture, forestry or horticulture degree and seek field placements, internships or entry-level estate roles.
Develop crop-specific knowledge where the target plantation sector requires it, such as tea culture and manufacture.
Progress through supervisory or assistant-management responsibilities before competing for manager-level estate posts.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The role combines technical crop-management capability with operational leadership. TRA Tocklai’s curriculum identifies practical tea-estate knowledge areas including agronomy, plant physiology and breeding, soil science, plant protection, processing, quality management, HR and grievance redressal, certification, statistics and market economics.

Transferable management skills include field assessment, site-specific planning, action-plan development, coordination with implementing partners, monitoring and reporting—capabilities specified in NHAI’s plantation-manager responsibilities.
bullets: Crop husbandry, soil and nutrient stewardship, irrigation or water-management awareness, and integrated pest and disease management.
Production, processing and quality-control literacy appropriate to the plantation crop.
Budgeting, record-keeping, statistics and market or cost awareness.
Team leadership, labour relations, communication, contractor coordination and problem-solving.
Field inspection, implementation monitoring and the ability to convert observations into an actionable site plan.

sections.heading: Workplace and operating context
paragraphs: Work is principally estate- and field-based rather than office-only. The Rajasthan Government career portal identifies tea plantations as the workplace for a Tea Plantation Manager; schedules can involve long days, five or six working days a week, shifts and overtime depending on the operation.

Managers in tea gardens operate in an industry where worker welfare is a material management context. The Ministry of Labour and Employment has stated that state governments implement welfare measures for tea-plantation workers and that the earlier Plantation Labour Act, 1951 has been subsumed into the Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020 and the Code on Social Security, 2020.
bullets: Expect regular outdoor field visits alongside administrative, staffing and production-coordination work.
The balance of field, factory and office work depends on the crop, estate scale and whether the employer also processes the crop.
Managers should understand the employer’s workplace-safety, welfare, wage and social-security obligations as applicable to the state and establishment.

sections.heading: Career progression
paragraphs: A typical progression is from plantation worker or field role to plantation supervisor, then Plantation Manager and General Manager. The Government of Rajasthan career portal presents this as indicative rather than guaranteed.

In practice, progression is strengthened by evidence of safely delivering crop plans, leading teams, maintaining quality and productivity, managing budgets and records, and handling larger or more complex estates. The Uttarakhand Tea Development Board’s Tea Estate Manager notice illustrates that senior roles may seek prior assistant-manager experience.
bullets: Entry field or estate role → Plantation Supervisor or Assistant Manager → Plantation Manager → General Manager or estate leadership.
Movement into specialist functions—such as production, quality, field advisory, sustainability or plantation-project management—may also be possible depending on the employer.
